posts.raw_content:
How a Chinese Company Broke American Criminal Law and FTC Regulations in Pursuit of Cheaters


This Is Not About Cheaters

This document is not a defense of video game cheaters. Game companies have every right to detect them and ban them. This is about a company that went beyond detection and banning, took offensive action against hardware it does not own, and in doing so violated existing federal law and established cybersecurity policy. The legal framework to address this already exists. It needs to be enforced.


What Riot Did

Riot Games is wholly owned by Tencent Holdings, a Chinese technology conglomerate. (1)(2) On May 19, 2026, Riot pushed an update to its Vanguard anti-cheat software, which runs at kernel level on every computer where it is installed. The update forced a security feature called IOMMU into aggressive enforcement mode on detected cheat hardware, corrupting the devices' firmware through repeated page faults. (3)(4) Because these devices connect to storage controllers, the corruption cascaded into blue screens, unrecognizable drives, and systems requiring complete OS reinstalls, with risks of permanent data loss. (5)
On May 21, Riot's official X account posted a photo of disabled hardware captioned: "Congrats to the owners of a brand new $6k paperweight." (6)(7) When backlash followed, they stated: "Vanguard does not damage hardware or disable your devices." (8) These two positions are not compatible.


What This Looks Like Outside of Gaming

Imagine the factory radio in your truck dies. You install a discount replacement that plugs into the vehicle's computer and requires you to buy music through their store. Your teenager borrows the truck, plugs in a USB stick full of music. Some ripped from CDs the family owns. Some from a streaming service that shut down two years ago. No licensing metadata on any of it. The radio cannot verify ownership and defaults to flagging everything as pirated.


In response, it deliberately disrupts the engine's ignition timing through the vehicle's computer. Bad timing causes misfires. Misfires cause overheating. The engine is destroyed. Thousands in repairs on a truck you are still making payments on. Your kid was not a pirate. The system could not prove otherwise and treated that as guilt.
The radio company posts: "Congrats on your new paperweight." When pressed: "We didn't damage the engine. We simply enforced a standard vehicle security feature. The damage was indirect."
No regulator would accept that. No court would accept it. The law does not contain an exception for "indirect" damage that was deliberately engineered and publicly celebrated.


This Violates Existing Law

The CFAA (18 U.S.C. § 1030) makes it a federal offense to knowingly transmit a program that intentionally or recklessly causes damage to a protected computer. "Damage" means "any impairment to the integrity or availability of data, a program, a system, or information." (9) Systems requiring reinstallation and drives becoming unrecognizable meet that definition. Riot's public celebration of the outcome eliminates any credible claim the damage was unintended.


Riot claims their Terms of Service authorizes everything. That Terms of Service is a contract of adhesion: non-negotiable, take-it-or-leave-it. Courts enforce adhesion contracts but refuse to enforce terms that are unconscionable. Putting something in a contract does not make it legal. An apartment building cannot add a lease clause stating that upon receiving a noise complaint, the maintenance crew may enter the unit and destroy any audio equipment, speakers, televisions, or sound systems. A landlord can write those words. A tenant can sign them. But the clause is unenforceable, and any maintenance worker who actually smashed a tenant's television would face misdemeanor destruction of property charges regardless of what the lease says. The signature does not create a license to destroy someone else's property. Riot's Terms of Service does not either.


The DOJ stated in 2015 that private offensive cyber operations are "generally unlawful" under the CFAA. Congress twice rejected the Active Cyber Defense Certainty Act (2017 and 2019), which would have created a narrow legal pathway for private offensive operations. (10)(11) Riot's conduct is precisely the type of operation existing law prohibits.
The FTC has independent authority under Section 5(n) of the FTC Act (15 U.S.C. § 45(n)). A practice is unfair if it causes substantial injury to consumers, the injury is not reasonably avoidable, and it is not outweighed by countervailing benefits. The FTC has shut down software operations for conduct far less destructive than this. In FTC v. Seismic Entertainment (2004), software that modified system settings and caused harm was charged as unfair. (16) In FTC v. ERG Ventures (2006), the FTC obtained a permanent injunction against software that was "intrusive, disruptive, and makes it difficult for consumers to use their computers," and specifically found that a deceptive EULA did not shield the company. (17) Riot's update meets the Section 5(n) test: the injury is substantial, not reasonably avoidable, and not outweighed by benefits since Riot has numerous less destructive options (account bans, hardware ID blacklists, service denial) that achieve the same goal.


False Positives Make This a Consumer Safety Issue

Vanguard has a documented history of flagging legitimate hardware. In August 2025, a user reported that after enabling IOMMU, their Mellanox ConnectX-3 network card and Blackmagic Decklink capture card both stopped functioning. Months of providing logs to Riot support produced no resolution. (12) Under the old model, a false positive meant a wrongful ban. Under the new model, it means a potentially corrupted operating system on a machine where no violation occurred. The initial targets were unsympathetic, likely chosen to establish the practice with minimal resistance. But the detection does not distinguish between intended targets and legitimate hardware it cannot verify, and the consequences of error are now system-level damage.


The Foreign Ownership Dimension

CFIUS has been reviewing Tencent's gaming investments since the first Trump administration. In early 2026, the Trump administration held cabinet-level meetings on whether to force Tencent to divest from Riot, Epic Games, and Supercell. The Biden administration previously added Tencent to a list of companies with alleged ties to the Chinese military. (13)(14)(15) The May 2026 update demonstrates that a Tencent subsidiary is not merely collecting data through kernel-level access. It is actively using that access to conduct offensive operations against hardware on American machines, and publicly advertising the capability.


Conclusion

A Chinese-owned company deployed kernel-level software on millions of American computers, deliberately engineered conditions that cascaded into system failures on hardware it does not own, publicly celebrated the destruction, and then claimed it caused no damage. It chose its first targets carefully: unsympathetic victims, selected to establish the practice with minimal pushback. The precedent is the product.
We are not going to live in a world where the radio in your car destroys your engine because it thinks you did not buy your music through its app store. We are not going to live in a world where streaming services brick televisions they did not sell. We are not going to live in a world where a Chinese conglomerate slips a precedent for remotely destroying American property it does not own through a video game.

The FTC needs to immediately pull this product. It is unlawful to sell software that does what Vanguard is doing, and the FTC has shut down companies for far less.


The individuals responsible should face consequences. If a maintenance worker walked into a tenant's apartment and smashed their stereo, television, and speakers over a noise complaint, that worker would face destruction of property charges regardless of what the lease says. The engineers who built this capability knew what it would do. The executives who approved it knew what it would do. The social media team that posted "congrats on your brand new $6k paperweight" knew what it had done. A Terms of Service does not immunize any of them, any more than a lease clause immunizes the maintenance worker swinging the hammer.
The law already prohibits this. It is time to enforce it against every person who made it happen.

References
(1) PC Gamer, "Every game company that Tencent has invested in," November 2024.

(2) TechCrunch, "Tencent Takes Full Control Of Riot Games," December 2015.

(3) Esports.gg, "Riot Games 'bricks' $6K DMA cheats? Vanguard update explained," May 22, 2026.

(4) TweakTown, "Valorant's Vanguard anti-cheat now destroys DMA cheat firmware," May 23, 2026.

(5) TalkEsport, "Riot Reportedly Remotely Disabling Users' Hardware After Latest Vanguard Update," May 23, 2026.

(6) GamerMarkt, "Vanguard Update Bricks DMA Cheat Devices," May 23, 2026.

(7) Dexerto, "Riot Games responds to backlash after boasting that Vanguard anti-cheat can 'brick' hardware," May 23, 2026.

(8) Shacknews, "Riot Games issues statement on Valorant Vanguard anti-cheat 'bricking' cheaters' PCs," May 23, 2026.

(9) 18 U.S.C. § 1030(e)(8), Computer Fraud and Abuse Act, definition of "damage."

(10) Defense Daily, "DoJ Official Says Private Hacking Back Illegal, Unhelpful," May 2015.

(11) Congress.gov, H.R.3270, 116th Congress (2019-2020), "Active Cyber Defense Certainty Act."

(12) AnswerOverflow / Valorant support forum, "Vanguard IOMMU/DMA protection - legitimate PCIe devices," August 2025.

(13) Tom's Hardware, "Trump administration weighs forcing China's Tencent to sell its stakes in Epic Games, Riot Games, and Supercell," March 4, 2026.

(14) WCCFTech, "The US Government Might Compel Tencent to Divest from US Gaming Companies for National Security," March 4, 2026.

(15) PYMNTS, "White House Reviews Tencent's Gaming Stakes as Trump Prepares for China Visit," March 4, 2026.

(16) Federal Trade Commission, "FTC Cracks Down on Spyware Operation" (FTC v. Seismic Entertainment Productions, Inc.), October 12, 2004.

(17) Federal Trade Commission, "FTC Permanently Halts Media Motor Spyware Scam" (FTC v. ERG Ventures, LLC), October 2007.
